speaker: interviewer
text: Your experiences in the war affected the way you brought up your children? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I want them to be honest, you know? I want them to be happy. I want them to love the family, to appreciate, you know, the family, to love each other. As I said, to be honest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still think about the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely, even after 50 years I'm still waking up at night, you know, screaming sometimes. My wife, you know, you know, jerks me, and says, Steve, Steve, what's the matter? What's the matter? You know? I said, those dogs are after me again, you know? I remember, you know, still. And especially when-- when they have on television, some kind of program about the Holocaust, my wife says, you know, don't watch it, Steve. Don't watch it, you know? Because you'll be dreaming. I said, no. I said, I-- it happens, you know, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned earlier that the lice used to eat at you. Do have scars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have a scar on my-- my foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If you can see it. That's from the lice. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do people ask you about that as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, they don't-- they don't see it, you know? I don't mention it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Besides your son asking you about your tattoo, has anybody else ever remarked on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my-- my-- my children, yeah. Well, yeah. Yes, my daughters, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the time when you actually did tell Mark about what that number meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that I recall it. I remember I had my-- my uniform, my prison uniform that when I was liberated in Buchwald. You know, I still had the-- and as a matter of fact, it's-- it's in-- in the museum now in-- in-- in-- West Bloomfield. You know, we have in Detroit, a Holocaust museum. My uniform is on display there. And thousands and thousands of children a year are going through this, you know, and they see my uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm glad I brought it, you know? People ask me why and how. Why did you do it? I said, I don't know. I just-- I just brought it. I said, maybe someday, I'll show it to my children, you know, to my family, to my wife or-- you know, to see, to-- they should see, you know? What believe in-- I can't understand that there's people today in this world that they-- that they say that the Holocaust never exists. I mean, they see the numbers on the hands, on the arms. And they see what we went through, and they hear-- they're talking to live people that they went through, and they still say that's, you know, there's no such a thing. I mean, I don't get that. I don't get it. I mean, you're just ignorant or just put the name in the headlines in the papers, I guess. That's about it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever been interviewed or told your story in its entirety to anyone before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for you coming forth today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I happened to notice it, I believe, it was in the-- in the in Detroit, you know, where-- where I live, you know? And I happened to note a little article about this year, and I called the place, and they told me, you know, about it. So I said, OK, I'll give my-- my interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we close, and go to the-- to the photographs, is there anything you'd like to add? Is there a message you have for someone who might be watching this, or your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I-- I-- first of all, I'd like to thank Steven Spielberg for making this, you know, this documentary. And-- and I'd like to thank my family. You know, and I hope that someday, you know, when they have the time or maybe 10 years, 20 years from today I won't be around to see, the-- you know, my life story, you know, what happened, you know just because I was Jewish, you know, and believe in God. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And I'm proud to-- that I am American citizen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That-- I'm glad that they-- they-- they came in time to-- to liberate me. And just I'm sorry, you know, I'm sad that my-- my-- my family couldn't, you know, make it through it. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] And I'd like to thank the American Army for-- for liberating me. They made it-- they made it just in time. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I'm glad I was around, you know, to-- to tell my-- my story here. Because today, five years, 10 years, I don't know what happen, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to thank my-- my family, you know, for being so nice. And I have a beautiful family, you know? And I wish them well, you know, and health and happiness and I hope they live a long life, you know, and they be happy. And-- and if-- if they can help somebody, other people, you know, let them help them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK, now sitting down on the left to right is my grandmother Gitel. My Zayde, grandfather, Motcha. My uncle, Leib. And now, from standing up from left to right is my mother, Bluma. My Tante, Aunt Bryna, and my uncle, Uncle Meyer. There's two sisters and two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was Ajzyk. Last name was Ajzyk. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, this is my mother. My mother's name was Bluma, and they used to call her [? Shayna ?] Bluma

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name was Ajzyk. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK to the left, standing up is my father, Moshe, Moses. And to my right standing up is my mother, Bluma. And the other people, I don't know who they are. That's the only thing I know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this picture is my-- for my-- is my father and mother. I don't know if they were married already at that time, or they were engaged. I don't know. But all those pictures were given to me by my uncle, here, from United States. The one who brought me down here to this country, you know? And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's Moshe and Bluma. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, this-- that's me. Maybe I was three, four years old. [? Slemahesh ?]. Good looking kid. [LAUGHS] Yeah, that was given to-- to me by my uncle. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, this is my family, too. There's my grandmother there, my grandfather. Next to my grandmother is my mother. And my mother's holding one of my sisters, Leiba, because the other one that had the name Gitel, she wasn't born yet. And then next to my Zayde, is my tante, then a cousin. And above is my uncle, Uncle Meyer. My Tante Leiba was Meyer's wife. And then there's my father. And then there's another uncle, which is Bryna's husband. They were from Denmark. And that's me in the front, sitting there. And another little cousin is holding my grandmother-- my Bubbe's holding little cousin, there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's about-- that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your grandmother and grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's grandmother, Gitel, and my grandfather, Motcha. And my aunt's name is Bryna, as I said before, you know? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my glass truck when I was in the glass business. And that's me in the front. And I believe there's a couple guys that used to work for me when I was in the glass business in-- in Detroit-- Detroit, Michigan. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The bottom picture is a-- a New Year's card. It was made. I sent to my friends. It was made in-- in Landsberg DP camp. I made New Year's cards. It was sent out for New Year's. On the right, that's me. And I don't know if this was made in the camp, in DP camp or not. I think it was at a photographer. And to the left on the top, that's me riding the bike. That wasn't in the camp. In the back is the [NON-ENGLISH], or the building, where I stayed in and that was from Landsberg. Landsberg [NON-ENGLISH] DP camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's me and my wife. And we got married, I believe, December the 27th of 1952. That's our-- Phyllis, my wife's name is Phyllis, and we've been married, up to now, to about 42. We're going on 43 and still strong. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, that's me and my wife, when we married, on the wedding date. And to the right this my Uncle Meyer. And to my left is my Tante Hannah. They were my interferers, you know, at the wedding. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, here's my son's family. Mark, Moshe, holding his-- my-- my grandson, Sam. Next to him is my granddaughter, Leah. My daughter-in-law, Carol. And next to my daughter, Carol, is Sarah. And to the left on the bottom is my grandson, Joe. And on the top is my grandson, Jason. That's my son's family. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my son-in-law, Paul and my daughter, Bonnie. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And here, that's me with all my seven grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I love them all. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK, this is my uniform that I was liberated in Buchenwald. And it was taken in-- in Detroit from one of the Detroit magazines. They came and took that picture, which my uniform is in the-- in the museum, in the Holocaust Museum in West Bloomfield. I believe they wrote a article about Holocaust, and they came and they took that picture. And this is my uniform. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This, is my wife, my beautiful wife. 42 years and still going strong. This is my daughter, Lisa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my-- my son-in-law Shawn. This is my granddaughter, Lindsey, my granddaughter, Cayla, twins, about-- a little bit over two years old. Beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would just like to read a couple of poems. One was written by my mother and one was written by my sister. And they basically tell a lot. I'll read them and you'll be able to tell that for yourself. This one was written by my mother. She is the most poetic one in the family. 'First thing in the morning as the sun rose in the sky, I heard the sound of tanks. I heard my mother cry. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change. I was taken from my home. Never to see my family again. Put into a work camp by an army of cruel men. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change. Then there were the concentration camps. The smell of burning flesh, the number tattooed on my arm, and to die a daily wish. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: The years pass so slowly. Will this hell go on forever? Then came the day of liberation, the American Army, the Savior. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change. When the outward scars were healed, where would my home be? Israel? Australia? Back to Poland? Or America, the home of the free. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made my new home in America, and became a citizen of the USA. Got married and raised a family, and gave thanks for every day. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change. Oh, the memories how they linger on. I cry out in my sleep. My life is so very good now, but my memories make me weep. And it only took a moment for my whole life to change.'

speaker: interviewee
text: She made it through. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And now this one is by my sister, who, oh, suffered her own atrocities, I guess. 'You silently sit--'

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my sister, Bonnie. 'You silently sit with your pain and your memories, and your story of surviving the Holocaust is told. Your screams when you yell out in the night pierce my heart, and I am continually reminded of the injustices done to you by that tattoo on your arm. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: The pain, it goes so deep, so deep down to the roots, the roots of my soul, the roots of my very existence. Who is my family anyways? Where are they? There are all the people who have also lost theirs, a common bond of pain and anger, mostly unexpressed, but passed on nonetheless. Where did you get your survival skills? From your parents, and they from theirs? And now I from you. When do you shed your tears? I hope that yours are as healing as mine.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would like to say that he also passed that survival on to me, as well. I think that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the suffering that he experienced has made me a more tolerant person, I think. And I-- I think that, although the Germans took away his family, they never took away his-- his will to live and his self respect and his dignity. And I think that he has passed that on to-- to me and the rest of my siblings. And I hope that I'm able to pass it on to my children. And I hope that they can pass it on to theirs.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the wife that didn't get their name mentioned. It's Phyllis. [LAUGHS] I just am so grateful that Steve was chosen to be a survivor and that I was chosen to be his wife because he's just one of the finest people around, and I love him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.
